topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box was heavy, smelling faintly of old pencil shavings and dried glue. Leo dragged it from the attic, the effort feeling like lifting a sack of wet sand. He’d been cleaning out the spare room, a task he’d put off for months, and this discovery had just surfaced. Inside, nestled amongst faded notebooks and crumpled worksheets, was an astonishing collection: every assignment from middle school, meticulously cataloged. He picked up a geometry proof from the eighth grade. The ink was faded, the handwriting neat, utterly devoid of any discernible emotion. Leo flipped through a stack of essays, his gaze skimming the topics—Shakespearean sonnets, historical analyses, biology diagrams—as if reading a grocery list. He didn't feel a surge of nostalgia or shock; just a dull sense of observation. He placed the box back down, the weight settling on his shoulders without any real impact. He decided, without much thought, to just put the box in the storage closet and move on to sorting old tax documents.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya mumbled, leaning against the kitchen counter, picking at a loose thread on her sweater. Her gaze drifted to the overflowing filing cabinet her mother had insisted on keeping. Tucked inside were binders labeled with dates and subjects, each containing a complete record of her academic life. Maya didn't move toward them. She just watched, her posture slumped, the movement minimal. A few of the papers fluttered out onto the linoleum floor. She bent down slowly, picking up a physics homework sheet. The equations swam before her eyes, meaningless symbols. There was no sudden burst of excitement, no sharp pang of recognition. It was just data. She smoothed the paper flat against her palm, a gesture purely mechanical. "Well," she said, her voice flat, "that’s… a lot of paper." She placed the sheet back in the pile without examining it further, turning instead to stare out the window at the unremarkable afternoon sky.

[story 3]

It was the sheer volume that caught Sarah’s attention. Her mother had compiled everything—every test, every essay, every participation grade—into a sprawling archive. Sarah walked into the study, the silence amplifying the rustle of old paper. She didn't approach the desk immediately. She stood by the doorway, her arms crossed loosely over her chest, observing the organized chaos. She ran a finger along the spine of a textbook from freshman year. The knowledge that her mother had preserved every single piece of her educational history registered as a simple fact, like noting the temperature outside. Sarah felt no urge to touch anything tenderly or to search for specific memories. She simply cataloged the arrangement, noting the consistency of the labeling system. After a minute of silent assessment, she turned and walked back to the living room, the heavy feeling of the discovery receding into the background noise of her everyday existence.
